title: Daily Conversation - 50 Common Phrases & Idioms
definition: These are the idioms and everyday phrases native speakers use constantly in casual conversation - at work, with friends, and in daily life. Knowing them helps you understand real conversations and sound more natural yourself.

idioms.meaning: to have the same understanding or opinion as someone else
idioms.example: Let's make sure we're on the same page before we start the project.

idioms.meaning: a way of wishing someone good luck, especially before a performance
idioms.example: Break a leg at your interview tomorrow!

idioms.meaning: to stay determined despite difficulties
idioms.example: I know it's tough right now, just hang in there.

idioms.meaning: to stop working on something for the rest of the day
idioms.example: We've done enough for today, let's call it a day.

idioms.meaning: to start something
idioms.example: Let's get the ball rolling on this project.

idioms.meaning: to force yourself to do something unpleasant or difficult
idioms.example: I finally bit the bullet and went to the dentist.

idioms.meaning: said when a person who was just being discussed arrives
idioms.example: Speak of the devil, here comes Raj now.

idioms.phrase: The ball is in your court
idioms.meaning: it's your turn to make a decision or take action
idioms.example: I've sent the proposal, so the ball is in your court now.

idioms.meaning: to miss an opportunity
idioms.example: If you don't apply now, you'll miss the boat.

idioms.meaning: to do something in the easiest or cheapest way, often lowering quality
idioms.example: They cut corners on the renovation and it shows.

idioms.meaning: something that seems bad at first but turns out to be good
idioms.example: Losing that job was a blessing in disguise.

idioms.meaning: what you do matters more than what you say
idioms.example: He promised to help, but actions speak louder than words.

idioms.meaning: to make a bad situation worse
idioms.example: First he was late, and to add insult to injury, he forgot the documents.

idioms.meaning: it's better to do something late than not at all
idioms.example: She finally apologized - better late than never.

idioms.meaning: to avoid getting to the point
idioms.example: Stop beating around the bush and tell me what happened.

idioms.phrase: By the skin of your teeth
idioms.meaning: to just barely succeed at something
idioms.example: We caught the flight by the skin of our teeth.

idioms.phrase: Cross that bridge when we come to it
idioms.meaning: to deal with a problem only when it actually happens, not before
idioms.example: We can plan for it later - let's cross that bridge when we come to it.

idioms.meaning: something that sounds simple but is actually difficult
idioms.example: Losing ten kilos sounds easy, but it's easier said than done.

idioms.phrase: Give someone the benefit of the doubt
idioms.meaning: to trust what someone says even without proof
idioms.example: I'll give him the benefit of the doubt this time.

idioms.meaning: to make a special effort to achieve something
idioms.example: She always goes the extra mile for her clients.

idioms.meaning: to go to bed
idioms.example: I'm exhausted, I'm going to hit the sack early tonight.

idioms.meaning: in the same difficult situation as someone else
idioms.example: Don't worry, we're all in the same boat with this deadline.

idioms.meaning: something isn't difficult to understand
idioms.example: Setting up the printer isn't rocket science.

idioms.meaning: to hope for good luck
idioms.example: Keep your fingers crossed that the weather stays good.

idioms.phrase: Kill two birds with one stone
idioms.meaning: to achieve two things with a single action
idioms.example: I can kill two birds with one stone by picking up groceries on my way home.

idioms.meaning: said before mentioning the final item, showing it's still important
idioms.example: And last but not least, I'd like to thank our sponsors.

idioms.meaning: being cautious after a bad experience
idioms.example: I don't trust that company anymore - once bitten, twice shy.

idioms.meaning: in a risky or precarious situation
idioms.example: After that mistake, he's on thin ice with his manager.

idioms.meaning: unexpectedly
idioms.example: She called me out of the blue after years of silence.

idioms.meaning: to calm down and control your emotions
idioms.example: Take a deep breath and pull yourself together before the meeting.

idioms.phrase: Put all your eggs in one basket
idioms.meaning: to risk everything on a single plan
idioms.example: It's risky to put all your eggs in one basket with just one client.

idioms.meaning: to agree with someone
idioms.example: We don't always see eye to eye, but we respect each other.

idioms.meaning: to avoid making a decision
idioms.example: Stop sitting on the fence and choose a side.

idioms.meaning: to reveal a secret
idioms.example: Come on, spill the beans - what happened at the meeting?

idioms.phrase: Take it with a grain of salt
idioms.meaning: to not fully believe something
idioms.example: I'd take his advice with a grain of salt, he's not an expert.

idioms.meaning: to enjoy the benefits of two different things at once
idioms.example: Working from home gives me the best of both worlds.

idioms.meaning: to think creatively, beyond the usual limits
idioms.example: We need to think outside the box to solve this problem.

idioms.meaning: time passes very quickly
idioms.example: I can't believe it's already December - time flies.

idioms.meaning: without attracting attention
idioms.example: The new policy went under the radar for most employees.

idioms.meaning: something in the past that is no longer worth worrying about
idioms.example: We had a disagreement last year, but it's water under the bridge now.

idioms.meaning: I completely agree
idioms.example: That was a tough meeting. - You can say that again.

idioms.phrase: Give someone a piece of your mind
idioms.meaning: to tell someone honestly, often angrily, what you think
idioms.example: I gave him a piece of my mind about being late again.

idioms.meaning: to become nervous about doing something you had planned
idioms.example: She got cold feet before her wedding speech.

idioms.meaning: to leave or start a journey
idioms.example: It's getting late, we should hit the road.

idioms.meaning: to join something popular
idioms.example: Everyone's jumping on the bandwagon with this new app.

idioms.meaning: to avoid bringing up a topic that could cause problems
idioms.example: Don't mention the argument again, just let sleeping dogs lie.

idioms.meaning: to have just enough money for basic needs
idioms.example: With rising prices, it's hard to make ends meet.

idioms.meaning: you must work hard or suffer to achieve something
idioms.example: Training for the marathon was tough, but no pain, no gain.

idioms.meaning: a promise to accept an offer at a later time
idioms.example: Can I take a rain check on lunch today? I'm swamped.

idioms.meaning: a small, visible part of a much larger problem
idioms.example: The layoffs are just the tip of the iceberg for what's coming.

quiz.question: 'On the same page' means:
quiz.explanation: It means everyone shares the same view or plan.

quiz.question: 'Bite the bullet' means:
options: to force yourself to do something unpleasant
quiz.answer: to force yourself to do something unpleasant
quiz.explanation: It means facing a difficult situation with courage.

quiz.question: Don't put all your eggs in one ___.
quiz.explanation: 'Put all your eggs in one basket' means risking everything on a single plan.

quiz.question: 'Spill the beans' means:
quiz.explanation: It means to reveal secret information, often accidentally.

quiz.question: 'Easier said than done' means:
options: something that sounds simple but is actually hard
quiz.answer: something that sounds simple but is actually hard
quiz.explanation: It highlights the gap between talking about doing something and actually doing it.
